g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Views in SQL. Let us begin by understanding what views are and why they matter in databases.
g.text: A Complete Guide to Virtual Tables in Database Systems

g.data-narration: A view is a virtual table defined by a stored SELECT query. It does not store data physically but derives results from underlying base tables.
g.text: A view is a virtual table based on the result of a SELECT query.
Unlike physical tables, views do NOT store data—they store the query definition.
When queried, the database executes the underlying SELECT and returns the result.

g.data-narration: Views provide data security by restricting access, simplify complex queries, and offer logical data independence from schema changes.
Physical tables store actual data on disk, while views only store query definitions that are executed at runtime to produce results.
SQL supports three main types of views: Simple views from one table, Complex views with aggregates or joins, and Materialized views with stored data.
Simple views are created from a single table without aggregates. They are always updatable and support INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations.
Complex views include multiple tables, aggregates, or GROUP BY. These views are typically read-only and cannot be updated directly.
g.text: • Aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX)

g.data-narration: Materialized views differ from regular views—they store actual query results on disk and must be periodically refreshed to update data.
g.text: • Store ACTUAL results on disk (physical storage)
• Must be REFRESHED periodically to sync with base tables
• Used in data warehousing for fast aggregations

g.data-narration: The CREATE VIEW syntax includes optional column list, the AS keyword, SELECT query, and optional WITH CHECK OPTION for data validation.
This example creates a view showing only IT department employees with their IDs, names, and salaries from the employees table.
g.text: CREATE VIEW it_employees AS SELECT emp_id, emp_name, salary FROM employees WHERE department = 'IT';

g.data-narration: The WITH CHECK OPTION ensures any DML operation through a view satisfies the view's WHERE condition, preventing data invisibility.
g.text: Ensures DML operations satisfy the WHERE condition:
• Prevents inserting rows that wouldn't appear in the view
• Prevents updating rows to values outside the view's filter
• Maintains data integrity and view visibility rules

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates how an UPDATE changing department to Sales fails because it violates the IT department filter in the view definition.
g.text: Example 2: WITH CHECK OPTION in Action
CREATE VIEW it_salary_view AS SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department='IT' WITH CHECK OPTION;
UPDATE it_salary_view SET department='Sales' WHERE emp_id=101; ❌ FAILS!
Row would become invisible → Update rejected!

g.data-narration: View updatability depends on several conditions: single base table, no aggregates, no GROUP BY, and direct column mapping without expressions.
This complex view with aggregate functions and GROUP BY creates a department salary summary that is read-only for reporting purposes.
g.text: SELECT department, COUNT(*) as total, AVG(salary) as avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY department;

g.data-narration: Views can also combine multiple tables using JOINs. This example joins employees with departments to create a unified employee-department view.
g.text: SELECT e.emp_id, e.emp_name, d.dept_name, e.salary FROM employees e JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id=d.id;

g.data-narration: Key points: views are virtual tables storing query definitions, simple views are updatable, complex views are read-only, and WITH CHECK OPTION ensures data integrity.
g.text: 1. View = virtual table → stores SELECT, not data
2. Simple views (single table, no aggregates) → updatable
3. Complex views (aggregates, JOINs, GROUP BY) → read-only
4. WITH CHECK OPTION → enforces WHERE condition on DML
5. Materialized views → physical storage, needs refresh
